title: Chapter 3: Negotiation Tactics for the Modern Apocalypse
content: They came at noon.

Roxanne was in the dean's office, reorganizing. She'd found a working espresso machine in the faculty lounge (someone had stocked it right before the world ended — bless their caffeine-addicted soul), a carton of oat milk that was still good, and a bag of whole-bean coffee that smelled like heaven.
She was making her third latte of the morning when Lena burst through the door.
"Roxy! There are people at the main gate. Armed people."
"The gate I bought?
"Yes! That gate! They're claiming they have authority to "inspect" the campus. They've got rifles."
Roxanne finished pouring her latte. Took a sip. Considered it.
"Not bad. Needs a little more foam."
"Roxy!"
"I heard you. Armed people at my gate. Let's go see what they want."
She grabbed her coffee and walked out. The Executioner fell into step behind her, chainsaw scraping a steady rhythm against the floor.
The main gate of Blackwood University was a wrought-iron masterpiece from the 1920s, all Gothic arches and ivy. Roxanne had always loved it. Now it was hers.
On the other side stood eight people. Four of them carried hunting rifles. One — a woman in her forties with a hard face and a military-style jacket — stood at the front, arms crossed.
Behind them, a pickup truck with a mounted spotlight. The spotlight flickered, running on a generator that coughed in the violet light.
Roxanne walked up to the gate. Took another sip of her latte.
"Can I help you?"
"You're the one who bought this campus," the woman said. It wasn't a question.
"I am. Roxanne Vance. Art student, former credit card debtor, current real estate mogul. And you are?"
"Commander Hayes. Salvation Coalition, Eastern Sector."
"Salvation Coalition. That's a fancy name for a group I've never heard of."
"We're the only organized resistance in this city. We've secured the hospital, the police station, and the water treatment plant. We provide protection and supplies to survivors."
"Protection. Right. And what does that protection cost?"
Hayes's eyes narrowed.
"We don't charge survivors. We ask for reasonable contributions to the collective effort."
"So you do charge them. You just don't call it that."
"We have rules. Protocols. You can't just buy a university and call it your territory. The Nether-Grid doesn't work like that."
"Actually," Roxanne said, pulling out her black card, "it does. The deed is filed. The campus is registered. I have the receipt."
She held up the card. It pulsed once, and a translucent screen materialized in the air between them:

*PROPERTY DEED: BLACKWOOD UNIVERSITY*
*— Owner: Roxanne Vance*
*— Purchase Price: 10,470,000 Obols*
*— Status: PAID IN FULL*
*— Nether-Grid Registration: ACTIVE*
"See? Paid in full. No liens. No mortgages. Just me."
Commander Hayes stared at the floating screen. Her jaw tightened.
"Where did you get that kind of currency?"
"Found it. Inherited it. It's complicated."
"Nothing in the Nether-Grid is free. That card — whatever it is — you're going to attract attention. Entities that will want it back. Powers that won't ask nicely."
Roxanne tilted her head. Studied Hayes's face. The woman wasn't just angry — she was scared. Genuinely, professionally scared.
"You know something," Roxanne said quietly. "About what's coming."
Hayes was silent for a long moment. Then she said: "There's a sealed gate beneath this campus. The Hollow Cathedral. Do you know what's in it?"
"I know it exists. I bought it as part of the deal."
"There's a god in there. A real one. Not one of the surface-level horrors — one of the old ones. The Bound God, they call it. The other Primordials sealed it there before humanity existed."
Roxanne's latte suddenly felt very warm in her hands.
"And?"

"And that card you're holding — it's Primordial. I can feel it from here. When you activated it, you sent a signal through the entire Nether-Grid. Every faction, every major entity, every god-tier power now knows there's a Primordial artifact in play."
Hayes stepped closer to the gate.
"The Bound God in that cathedral? It felt it too. And if it wakes up — if someone or something wakes it up — there won't be a Salvation Coalition. There won't be a city. There won't be anything."
Roxanne took another sip of her latte. Let the silence stretch.
Then she smiled.
"Good."
"Good?" Hayes stared at her. "Did you hear what I just said?"
"I heard you. Primordial artifact. Bound god. Signal sent. Old powers stirring. That's the sales pitch."
"The — the what?"
Roxanne set down her latte on the gate's stone pillar. Pulled out the black card. Held it up so the violet light caught its obsidian surface.

"You came here to warn me. Or recruit me. Or intimidate me. I haven't figured out which yet. But here's the thing, Commander Hayes: I'm not looking for protection. I'm not looking to join your coalition. I'm the one doing the hiring."
She tapped the card against the gate.
*DISPLAY: SALVATION COALITION — EASTERN SECTOR — CURRENT DEBTS*
The screen flickered and a list appeared:

*— Emergency Generator Lease: 8,000 Obols/month (2 months overdue)*
*— Water Purification Contract: 12,000 Obols/month (3 months overdue)*
*— Protection Subcontract (Nether-Grid): 250,000 Obols (lien against territory)*

"You're in debt," Roxanne said. "Deep debt. That Nether-Grid protection subcontract? Someone's charging you a quarter million Obols to keep the really scary things off your turf. And you're falling behind."
Hayes's face went white.
"How do you know that?"
"I can see prices. It's a side effect of the card."
Roxanne stepped forward, opened the gate, and walked through. The Executioner followed. Hayes's soldiers raised their rifles.
The Executioner's chainsaw revved once.
The rifles lowered.

"I'm going to make you an offer," Roxanne said, stopping in front of Hayes. "I'll buy out your Nether-Grid protection contract. I'll cover your generator lease. I'll even throw in a water purification upgrade for the hospital. In exchange, you acknowledge this campus as sovereign territory. No Coalition interference. No 'inspections.' No 'contributions.'"
Hayes stared at her.
"Why would you do that?"
Roxanne shrugged.

"Because I don't want to be the only safe place in the city. That's a lot of pressure. And because you're the only other organized group that isn't actively trying to eat people. That's worth something."
She extended her hand.
"Deal?"
Hayes took her hand.
The deal was sealed.

An hour later, the Coalition convoy pulled away from the main gate, carrying a newly upgraded water filtration system, a paid generator lease receipt, and a very confused Commander who wasn't sure if she'd just been helped, outmaneuvered, or both.
Roxanne watched them go from the dean's office window.
"You just spent, like, three hundred thousand Obols," Lena said from behind her.

"Two hundred seventy-eight thousand," Roxanne corrected. "And I bought myself breathing room. The Coalition won't bother us. The Nether-Grid protection contract covers both their territory and mine now. And that commander? She'll remember that I could have crushed her and chose not to. That's worth more than any contract."
"That's... actually kind of terrifying."
"Thank you."
Lena shook her head. "No, I mean — you've had this card for less than twenty-four hours. You've bought a campus, a monster, and a military alliance. What are you going to do tomorrow?"
Roxanne looked out at the violet sky. At the campus that was now hers. At the strange, terrifying, magnificent new world.
"Tomorrow," she said, "I'm going to find out what's in that cathedral basement. And I'm going to figure out who the Bound God is."
The black card pulsed once in her pocket.
Almost like it was answering.
*End of Chapter 3*
